humanoids.physical: A protective forest spirit, usually shown with blue skin, green hair, and small horns, able to take human form.
humanoids.stories: The abada guards the forest and its creatures, aiding respectful hunters and punishing those who waste or destroy.
humanoids.symbolism: The abada embodies the guardianship of the wild and the balance between humans and the forest.
humanoids.powers: Forest protection, shapeshifting, and the granting of luck to worthy hunters.
humanoids.modernRefs: Turkic folklore, Central Asian mythology studies.
humanoids.story2: Hunters who took only what they needed and honored the forest were said to win the abada's quiet favor.
humanoids.story3: The abada belongs to a wide Turkic tradition of forest guardians who keep the balance of the wild.

quiz.question: The abada is a guardian spirit of what?

humanoids.name: Astomi
humanoids.physical: A legendary race of people with no mouths, covered in hair, who never eat or drink.
humanoids.stories: The astomi were said to survive solely by inhaling the scent of fruits, flowers, and roots.
humanoids.symbolism: The astomi embody ancient wonder at the edges of the known world and the marvels imagined to dwell there.
humanoids.powers: Survival without food or drink, sustained entirely by pleasant scents.
humanoids.modernRefs: Pliny's Natural History, medieval bestiaries, the 'monstrous races' tradition.
humanoids.story2: Pliny the Elder described the astomi living near the source of the Ganges, sustained by smell alone.
humanoids.story3: Too strong an odor, it was said, could kill the delicate astomi, who guarded themselves against foul smells.

quiz.question: How do the astomi survive?

humanoids.name: Anung Ite
humanoids.physical: A spirit with two faces, one beautiful and one hideous, with spikes protruding from her elbows.
humanoids.stories: Once a vain woman punished with a second, monstrous face, Anung Ite became a powerful and dangerous spirit.
humanoids.symbolism: Anung Ite embodies the duality of beauty and ugliness and the consequences of vanity and ambition.
humanoids.powers: Dual nature, dangerous gifts, and the bestowing of skills like quillwork that come at a price.
humanoids.modernRefs: Lakota folklore, Native American mythology studies.
humanoids.story2: Anung Ite is said to teach women crafts such as quillwork, but her gifts can carry hidden dangers.
humanoids.story3: In some tales she is the wife of the trickster Iktomi, the two scheming together across Lakota lore.

quiz.question: What is unusual about Anung Ite?

humanoids.name: Asin
humanoids.physical: An ogre-like monster, often a hideous old woman carrying a great basket on her back.
humanoids.stories: Asin sneaks up on disobedient children, throwing them into her basket to carry home and devour.
humanoids.symbolism: Asin embodies the cautionary monster used to warn children away from danger and disobedience.
humanoids.powers: Stealth, strength, and the snatching of wandering or naughty children.
humanoids.modernRefs: Pacific Northwest folklore, cautionary tales.
humanoids.story2: Parents told of the Basket Woman to keep children from wandering off alone into the dangerous wilds.
humanoids.story3: In many versions, clever children escape Asin's basket by tricking her or cutting their way free.

quiz.question: What does Asin, the Basket Woman, carry?

humanoids.name: Baykok
humanoids.physical: An emaciated, skeletal flying monster with thin translucent skin and glowing eyes in hollow sockets.
humanoids.stories: The baykok is the ghost of a person cursed for terrible deeds, hunting warriors in the forest with invisible arrows.
humanoids.symbolism: The baykok embodies the restless, vengeful dead and the consequences of a life of wrongdoing.
humanoids.powers: Flight, invisibility, paralyzing arrows, and the consuming of its victims' life-essence.
humanoids.modernRefs: Anishinaabe folklore, Native American mythology studies.
humanoids.story2: The baykok is said to strike its prey with invisible arrows, then feed on their liver to sustain itself.
humanoids.story3: Its rattling, skeletal flight and glowing eyes made the baykok a dreaded figure of the northern night.

quiz.question: The baykok is the ghost of a person cursed for what?

humanoids.name: Biboon
humanoids.physical: An old man personifying winter, descending from the north to cover the land in snow and ice.
humanoids.stories: Biboon brings the cold and snow each year, retreating only when the spring spirit returns to thaw the land.
humanoids.symbolism: Biboon embodies the season of winter, the harsh north, and the eternal cycle of cold and renewal.
humanoids.powers: Control of snow, ice, and freezing winds, and the bringing of winter to the land.
humanoids.modernRefs: Anishinaabe folklore, seasonal myth studies.
humanoids.story2: In a classic tale, Biboon's power fades as the young spring spirit enters his lodge, melting the snow around them.
humanoids.story3: His yearly return and retreat mark the turning of the seasons in Anishinaabe storytelling.

quiz.question: Biboon personifies which season?

humanoids.name: Boo Hag
humanoids.physical: A witch-like creature that sheds her skin and rides victims in their sleep to feed on their breath and energy.
humanoids.stories: The boo hag slips into homes at night to 'ride' a sleeper, draining their life-force before dawn.
humanoids.symbolism: The boo hag embodies the terror of sleep paralysis and the draining of vitality by an unseen visitor.
humanoids.powers: Skin-shedding, breath-stealing, and the riding of sleepers to drain their energy.
humanoids.modernRefs: Gullah folklore, the saying 'don't let the hag ride you', Southern horror tradition.
humanoids.story2: A brimstone-painted door or a broom by the bed was said to delay the boo hag by forcing her to count straws until dawn.
humanoids.story3: If a boo hag rides someone too hard, the victim may be found dead, drained of breath by morning.

quiz.question: The boo hag feeds on a sleeper's what?

humanoids.name: Buffalo People
humanoids.physical: Shapeshifting beings who took human and buffalo form, an ancient people of the earth before humankind.
humanoids.stories: The Buffalo People intermarried with the gods to create the first humans, later becoming the buffalo themselves.
humanoids.symbolism: The Buffalo People embody the sacred bond between humans and the buffalo and the origins of the people.
humanoids.powers: Shapeshifting between human and buffalo, ancient wisdom, and a role in the creation of humankind.
humanoids.modernRefs: Siouan mythology, Plains tribal tradition.
humanoids.story2: In myth, the Buffalo People made peace with humankind, gifting them the earth and becoming the buffalo herds.
humanoids.story3: Several great figures of Siouan myth, including Anung Ite, are counted among the Buffalo People.

quiz.question: The Buffalo People eventually became what?

humanoids.name: Faceless Spirit
humanoids.physical: A spirit appearing as a young maiden with no features upon her face, carrying a basket.
humanoids.stories: The faceless spirit gathers the life-force of dead things in her basket, returning it to the Creator to renew life.
humanoids.symbolism: The faceless spirit embodies the recycling of life and the sacred role of death in renewing creation.
humanoids.powers: The gathering and returning of life-essence and the renewal of life from death.
humanoids.modernRefs: Iroquois folklore, Native American mythology studies.
humanoids.story2: She is said to collect the spent life of dead creatures, carrying it back so the Creator can make new life.
humanoids.story3: Her featureless face marks her as a being beyond ordinary identity, a servant of the great cycle of life.

quiz.question: What does the faceless spirit collect in her basket?

humanoids.name: Fiura
humanoids.physical: A small, ugly woman with a hideous appearance yet an irresistible, seductive power over men.
humanoids.stories: The Fiura bathes in waterfalls and seduces or curses men in the forest, driving the unfaithful or unwary to madness.
humanoids.symbolism: The Fiura embodies the dangerous, untamed seduction of the wild and the price of straying.
humanoids.powers: Irresistible seduction, the breath of madness, and the cursing of men and animals.
humanoids.modernRefs: Chilote folklore, Chilean literature.
humanoids.story2: In Chilote myth, the Fiura is sometimes called the wife or companion of the seductive forest dwarf, the Trauco.
humanoids.story3: Her breath was said to twist the faces of those who offended her and to sicken livestock.

quiz.question: The Fiura is associated with which other Chilote creature?

humanoids.name: Inipi
humanoids.physical: A shapeshifting ghost of a human, appearing normal or as a skeleton with extremely long nails.
humanoids.stories: The inipi begins to walk once a person's death is assured, taking many forms as it wanders.
humanoids.symbolism: The inipi embodies the restless soul and the uncertain boundary between the living and the dead.
humanoids.powers: Shapeshifting into any form, ghostly presence, and an existence between life and death.
humanoids.modernRefs: Kawaiisu folklore, Native American mythology studies.
humanoids.story2: Folklore says the inipi may be fully aware or wander obliviously, simply going through the motions of life.
humanoids.story3: To drive one off, a person was said to blow across their own open palm in its direction.

quiz.question: When does the inipi begin to walk?
options: Once a person's death is assured

humanoids.name: Kalku
humanoids.physical: A sorcerer of dark magic, human in appearance, who commands crows and harmful spirits.
humanoids.stories: The kalku practices black magic from caves, sending curses and controlling the imp-like creatures it keeps.
humanoids.symbolism: The kalku embodies the misuse of spiritual power and the dark counterpart to the healing machi.
humanoids.powers: Dark sorcery, control of crows and spirits, curses, and command of the creature called the imbunche.
humanoids.modernRefs: Mapuche folklore, Chilote legend, fantasy fiction.
humanoids.story2: The kalku is said to guard its cave with the imbunche, a twisted creature made from a stolen child.
humanoids.story3: The kalku stands opposed to the machi, the Mapuche healer who works good magic for the community.

quiz.question: The kalku is a sorcerer who controls which birds?

humanoids.name: Little People
humanoids.cultural: Cherokee, Shoshone, and many other nations
humanoids.physical: Tiny human-like beings, ranging from a few inches to a couple of feet tall, sometimes beautiful and sometimes wild.
humanoids.stories: The Little People dwell in forests, rivers, and caves, helping the kind, punishing the rude, and guarding the land.
humanoids.symbolism: The Little People embody the hidden spirits of the land and the respect owed to the unseen folk of nature.
humanoids.powers: Invisibility, nature magic, healing, mischief, and the granting of luck or misfortune.
humanoids.modernRefs: Cherokee and Plains folklore, Native American mythology studies.
humanoids.story2: The Cherokee Yunwi Tsunsdi were said to help lost children and teach medicine, but demanded their privacy be respected.
humanoids.story3: Many nations across North America share traditions of small, powerful folk dwelling in the wild places.

quiz.question: The Little People are spirits of what?

humanoids.name: Mesingw
humanoids.physical: A hairy guardian of the forest wearing a wooden mask, half red and half black, riding upon a great stag.
humanoids.stories: Mesingw is the keeper of the forest animals, ensuring respectful hunting and the balance of the wild.
humanoids.symbolism: Mesingw embodies the guardianship of game animals and the sacred reciprocity between hunter and forest.
humanoids.powers: Command of the forest animals, control of the hunt, and the blessing or denial of game.
humanoids.modernRefs: Lenape folklore, Native American mythology studies.
humanoids.story2: Hunters honored Mesingw to ensure success and balance, for he could grant or withhold the forest's game.
humanoids.story3: His distinctive mask, half red and half black, was sacred in Lenape ceremony and tradition.

quiz.question: Mesingw is the guardian of what?

humanoids.name: Sabuqwanilnu
humanoids.region: Northeastern North America and Maritime Canada
humanoids.physical: Mermaid-like beings, human above and fish below, dwelling in the waters between worlds.
humanoids.stories: These water people control and predict the weather, and can travel between the human world and the underworld through water.
humanoids.symbolism: The halfway people embody the power of water as a passage between worlds and the forecasting of the seas.
humanoids.powers: Weather control and prediction, travel between worlds, and transformation of those they marry.
humanoids.modernRefs: Mi'kmaq folklore, Algonquian mythology studies.
humanoids.story2: Algonquian lore tells of one of these water people taking a human husband, transforming him into one of their own kind.
humanoids.story3: Sailors and fishers heeded the songs of the halfway people, reading them as forecasts of coming storms.

quiz.question: The halfway people are able to control and predict what?

humanoids.name: Stikini
humanoids.physical: A witch that transforms into a monstrous owl at night after vomiting up its own heart and organs.
humanoids.stories: The stikini lives as a human by day, but at night it disgorges its insides, hangs them in a tree, and flies as a were-owl to feed on hearts.
humanoids.symbolism: The stikini embodies the hidden evil within the community and the horror of self-transformation.
humanoids.powers: Transformation into a were-owl, flight, and the devouring of human hearts.
humanoids.modernRefs: Seminole folklore, Native American horror tradition.
humanoids.story2: Folklore says a stikini can be destroyed by finding and treating its hung-up organs while it is in owl form.
humanoids.story3: Hearing the stikini's distinctive call in the night was a dire omen among the Seminole.

quiz.question: The stikini transforms into what animal?

humanoids.name: Tariaksuq
humanoids.physical: Caribou-like people existing in a parallel world, seen only as shadows or glimpses out of the corner of the eye.
humanoids.stories: The tariaksuq live as their own society in a world beside ours, becoming fully visible only when killed.
humanoids.symbolism: The tariaksuq embody the unseen world alongside our own and the mystery glimpsed at the edge of sight.
humanoids.powers: Near-invisibility, existence in a parallel world, and elusive presence.
humanoids.modernRefs: Inuit folklore, Arctic mythology studies.
humanoids.story2: Hunters said they could sense a tariaksuq nearby but could never look at one directly, only catch a fleeting shadow.
humanoids.story3: It was said a tariaksuq became fully visible to human eyes only at the moment of its death.

quiz.question: The tariaksuq can usually only be seen how?
options: As shadows from the corner of the eye

humanoids.name: Towin
humanoids.physical: A female spirit who guards the road to the afterlife, judging the souls who pass her lodge.
humanoids.stories: Souls travelling the spirit road stop at Towin's lodge, where she judges their worthiness to continue to the land of the dead.
humanoids.symbolism: Towin embodies the threshold of the afterlife and the judgment of a life's worth.
humanoids.powers: Judgment of souls, guardianship of the spirit road, and passage to the afterlife.
humanoids.modernRefs: Lakota folklore, Native American mythology studies.
humanoids.story2: Worthy souls are permitted by Towin to journey on to Wanagi Tamakoce, the Lakota land of spirits.
humanoids.story3: Her name is said to mean Blue Woman, though it can also be translated as Aunt.

quiz.question: Towin guards the road to what?

humanoids.name: Wechuge
humanoids.physical: A person possessed and overwhelmed by an ancient monstrous power, becoming a cannibal of immense strength.
humanoids.stories: The wechuge arises when a person is overcome by a primordial being, turning 'too strong' and craving human flesh.
humanoids.symbolism: The wechuge embodies the loss of self to monstrous power and the breaking of sacred taboos.
humanoids.powers: Superhuman strength, near-invulnerability, and an insatiable cannibalistic hunger.
humanoids.modernRefs: Athabaskan folklore, comparative study with the Wendigo.
humanoids.story2: Like the Wendigo, the wechuge is tied to cannibalism and the harsh winters of the northern forests.
humanoids.story3: Folklore says a wechuge can sometimes be cured if its monstrous power is drawn out before it fully takes hold.

quiz.question: A person becomes a wechuge by being overwhelmed by what?

humanoids.name: Yacuruna
humanoids.physical: Hairy water beings with deformed feet and heads turned backwards, riding crocodiles in the underwater world.
humanoids.stories: The yacuruna rule a mirror-world beneath the rivers, luring humans down to live among them and become yacuruna themselves.
humanoids.symbolism: The yacuruna embody the hidden world beneath the waters and the seductive danger of the river depths.
humanoids.powers: Mastery of the underwater realm, shapeshifting, seduction, and the transformation of humans.
humanoids.modernRefs: Amazonian folklore, Peruvian literature, ayahuasca tradition.
humanoids.story2: Their underwater city is said to be a reversed mirror of the human world, where everything is turned about.
humanoids.story3: Amazonian shamans speak of the yacuruna as keepers of powerful knowledge in the river's depths.

quiz.question: Where do the yacuruna dwell?

humanoids.name: Black-eyed Children
humanoids.physical: Children with pale skin and completely solid black eyes, appearing at doors and car windows at night.
humanoids.stories: The black-eyed children beg to be let in, and those who relent are said to fall gravely ill or worse.
humanoids.symbolism: The black-eyed children embody the modern dread of corrupted innocence and the danger of the threshold.
humanoids.powers: An unsettling compulsion that urges victims to grant them entry, and a deeply ominous presence.
humanoids.modernRefs: Internet creepypasta, paranormal media, modern urban legend.
humanoids.story2: The legend spread through the 1990s and the internet age, with reports of the children appearing at night asking for entry.
humanoids.story3: Folklore warns that, as with vampires, the black-eyed children must be invited in before they can do harm.

quiz.question: What is the defining feature of the black-eyed children?

humanoids.name: Hat Man
humanoids.physical: A living shadow in the shape of a man, often wearing a wide-brimmed or top hat, with glowing red eyes.
humanoids.stories: The Hat Man is reported during sleep paralysis, standing silently in doorways or at the foot of the bed.
humanoids.symbolism: The Hat Man embodies the modern shadow-figure of sleep paralysis and nameless nocturnal dread.
humanoids.powers: A paralyzing, oppressive presence, silent menace, and appearance during the half-waking state.
humanoids.modernRefs: Sleep-paralysis research, paranormal media, the shadow-people phenomenon.
humanoids.story2: Strikingly, people across the world report the same hatted shadow figure during episodes of sleep paralysis.
humanoids.story3: The Hat Man belongs to the broader shadow-people phenomenon, dark humanoid figures seen at the edge of sleep.

quiz.question: The Hat Man is most often reported during what?

humanoids.name: Blemmyes
humanoids.region: North Africa, Nubia (as recorded by Greek writers)
humanoids.physical: A legendary race of people without heads, their eyes and mouth set into their chests.
humanoids.stories: Ancient and medieval writers placed the headless blemmyes in the deserts and lands beyond Egypt.
humanoids.symbolism: The blemmyes embody ancient wonder at the unknown edges of the world and the marvels imagined there.
humanoids.powers: Survival as a headless race, dwelling in distant deserts beyond the known world.
humanoids.modernRefs: Pliny, Mandeville's Travels, Shakespeare's Othello, the 'monstrous races' tradition.
humanoids.story2: Shakespeare alludes to the headless blemmyes, 'men whose heads do grow beneath their shoulders', in Othello.
humanoids.story3: The name was also borne by a real Nubian people, whose distance lent itself to fantastical legend.

quiz.question: Where are the blemmyes' facial features located?

humanoids.name: Cynocephalus
humanoids.region: Africa and India (as recorded by Greek writers)
humanoids.physical: A race of people with human bodies and the heads of dogs, who bark or speak in their own tongue.
humanoids.stories: Ancient writers described dog-headed peoples in distant Africa and India, and medieval legend made Saint Christopher one.
humanoids.symbolism: The cynocephali embody the boundary between human and animal and the strangeness of distant peoples.
humanoids.powers: Strength, keen senses, and a place between the human and animal worlds.
humanoids.modernRefs: Pliny, medieval bestiaries, the legend of Saint Christopher.
humanoids.story2: In Eastern Christian tradition, Saint Christopher is sometimes depicted as a dog-headed cynocephalus before his conversion.
humanoids.story3: Travel writers from antiquity through the Middle Ages placed the dog-headed peoples at the edges of the known world.

quiz.question: The cynocephalus has the head of what animal?

humanoids.name: Mummy
humanoids.cultural: Ancient Egyptian tradition and modern legend
humanoids.physical: A preserved, bandage-wrapped corpse, sometimes said to rise and walk if its tomb is disturbed.
humanoids.stories: Egyptian mummies were preserved for the afterlife, and legend says some rise to curse and pursue tomb-robbers.
humanoids.symbolism: The mummy embodies the preservation of the dead, the sanctity of the tomb, and the curse of disturbing the past.
humanoids.powers: Reanimation in legend, the bringing of curses, and relentless pursuit of those who defile the tomb.
humanoids.modernRefs: The Mummy films, the 'curse of the pharaohs', countless horror media.
humanoids.story2: The legend of the 'curse of the pharaohs' grew after the opening of Tutankhamun's tomb in the 1920s.
humanoids.story3: In ancient belief, mummification preserved the body so the soul could live on in the afterlife.

quiz.question: The mummy legend originates from the burial practices of which civilization?

humanoids.name: Struthopodes
humanoids.region: Africa and India (as recorded by Greek writers)
humanoids.physical: A legendary race whose men had enormous feet and whose women had tiny ones, using their feet as shade.
humanoids.stories: Ancient writers described these far-off people resting on their backs and shading themselves beneath their great feet.
humanoids.symbolism: The struthopodes embody the playful marvels imagined at the edges of the ancient known world.
humanoids.powers: Great speed and the use of their oversized feet as parasols against the sun.
humanoids.modernRefs: Pliny's Natural History, medieval 'monstrous races' lore.
humanoids.story2: The struthopodes are kin to the sciapods, the one-legged people said to shade themselves under a single huge foot.
humanoids.story3: Such fantastic races filled ancient and medieval maps where real knowledge of the world gave out.

quiz.question: The struthopodes were said to use their feet as what?

humanoids.name: Shade
humanoids.physical: The dim, ghostly spirit of a dead person, a faint echo of the living dwelling in the shadowy underworld.
humanoids.stories: Shades reside in the gloomy land of the dead, sometimes summoned or encountered by heroes who descend to the underworld.
humanoids.symbolism: The shade embodies the lingering remnant of the soul and the dim, sorrowful nature of the afterlife.
humanoids.powers: Spectral presence, prophecy when summoned, and the faint memory of a former life.
humanoids.modernRefs: The Odyssey's underworld, classical and fantasy literature.
humanoids.story2: In the Odyssey, Odysseus journeys to the underworld and speaks with the shades of the dead, who crowd to drink offered blood.
humanoids.story3: The concept appears across the Near East and Mediterranean, from Mesopotamian spirits to the Choctaw Nalusa Chito.

quiz.question: A shade is the spirit of what, residing in the underworld?

humanoids.name: Monopod
humanoids.region: Libya and India (as recorded by Greek writers)
humanoids.physical: A one-legged people with a single enormous foot, on which they hop with surprising speed.
humanoids.stories: Ancient writers said the monopods rested on their backs in the heat, raising their great foot as a sunshade.
humanoids.symbolism: The monopod embodies the imaginative wonders placed at the far edges of the ancient world.
humanoids.powers: Swift hopping movement and the use of a single huge foot for shade.
humanoids.modernRefs: Pliny, medieval maps and bestiaries, The Chronicles of Narnia's Dufflepuds.
humanoids.story2: C.S. Lewis reimagined the monopods as the comical, hopping Dufflepuds in The Voyage of the Dawn Treader.
humanoids.story3: Medieval cartographers drew the one-footed sciapods at the margins of the known world, shading beneath their soles.

quiz.question: How many legs does a monopod have?

humanoids.name: Kakamora
humanoids.physical: Small, hairy forest-dwelling people with long sharp nails and large eyes, living in caves and trees.
humanoids.stories: The kakamora dwell in the deep forest, generally shy but able to stab intruders with their long claws.
humanoids.symbolism: The kakamora embody the hidden little folk of the islands and the mystery of the deep forest.
humanoids.powers: Stealth, agility, climbing, and long sharp claws used in defense.
humanoids.modernRefs: Solomon Islands folklore, the film Moana's coconut-armored kakamora.
humanoids.story2: Islanders describe the kakamora as fearing the color white and dwelling far from human villages.
humanoids.story3: The film Moana drew loosely on the kakamora, reimagining them as tiny coconut-armored pirates.

quiz.question: The kakamora come from the folklore of which islands?

humanoids.name: Junjudee
humanoids.physical: A small, brown, hairy man of the bush, strong for his size and impervious to ordinary weapons.
humanoids.stories: The junjudee roams the bush of southeastern Queensland, mischievous and at times dangerous to those it meets.
humanoids.symbolism: The junjudee embodies the little wild folk of the Australian bush and its hidden dangers.
humanoids.powers: Great strength for its size, near-invulnerability to weapons, and stealth.
humanoids.modernRefs: Australian Aboriginal folklore, cryptid lore.
humanoids.story2: Sometimes considered a smaller relative of the yowie, the junjudee is one of the bush's little hairy folk.
humanoids.story3: Stories describe the junjudee as mischievous, but it could turn dangerous to those who crossed it.

quiz.question: The junjudee is a small hairy man of which land?

humanoids.name: Mumari
humanoids.physical: A hairy creature of the bush, regarded as an evil spirit that stalks people through the night.
humanoids.stories: The mumari follows a lone traveller home through the dark, trying to catch them before they reach safety.
humanoids.symbolism: The mumari embodies the menace of the night bush and the dread of being followed in the dark.
humanoids.powers: Stealth, persistence, and the stalking of victims through the night.
humanoids.modernRefs: Australian Aboriginal folklore, cautionary tales.
humanoids.story2: Tales of the mumari warned travellers not to walk the bush alone after dark, lest the spirit follow them home.
humanoids.story3: The mumari belongs to a wider Aboriginal tradition of hairy bush spirits and night-stalking beings.

quiz.question: The mumari is said to do what to a lone traveller?
options: Follow and try to catch them

humanoids.name: Tall Man
humanoids.physical: A towering, malevolent being that emerges at night from cracks in rocks or the shadows of the rainforest.
humanoids.stories: The Tall Man stalks the tropical rainforests of North Queensland, a nightmare figure to be avoided at all costs.
humanoids.symbolism: The Tall Man embodies the deep dread of the rainforest night and the malevolent spirits of the shadows.
humanoids.powers: Towering, looming presence, emergence from shadow and stone, and the instilling of terror.
humanoids.modernRefs: Aboriginal folklore, the Quinkan rock-art tradition, cryptid lore.
humanoids.story2: The Tall Man is linked in some traditions to the Quinkan spirits depicted in ancient Cape York rock art.
humanoids.story3: Elders warned that the Tall Man comes out at night, and that the rainforest shadows are no place to wander alone.

quiz.question: From where does the Tall Man emerge at night?
options: Cracks in rocks and rainforest shadows

humanoids.name: Homunculus
humanoids.physical: A tiny, fully-formed artificial human grown by an alchemist, no taller than a hand.
humanoids.stories: Alchemists such as Paracelsus claimed a homunculus could be cultivated in a sealed vessel, then nurtured until it became a loyal, knowing little servant.
humanoids.symbolism: The homunculus embodies humanity's ambition to create life through art and science rather than birth.
humanoids.powers: Hidden knowledge, obedience to its maker, and an uncanny awareness beyond its tiny size.
humanoids.modernRefs: Fullmetal Alchemist, gothic fiction, video games, occult art.
humanoids.story2: Paracelsus left a recipe claiming a homunculus could be raised in a flask, fed with blood, and taught secret arts.
humanoids.story3: Legends warn that a homunculus, though loyal, resents its bottled birth and may turn on a careless creator.

quiz.question: How is a homunculus traditionally said to be created?
options: Grown through alchemy in a vessel

humanoids.name: Basajaun
humanoids.physical: A towering, shaggy-haired wild man of the forests, immensely strong and covered in hair.
humanoids.stories: Called the Lord of the Woods, the Basajaun guards flocks and forests, and folklore credits him with teaching humans agriculture, milling, and ironworking.
humanoids.symbolism: The Basajaun represents the untamed wisdom of the wilderness and the gifts nature offers to humankind.
humanoids.powers: Immense strength, guardianship of herds, and ancient knowledge of crafts and the land.
humanoids.modernRefs: Basque cultural revival, fantasy fiction, cryptid discussions.
humanoids.story2: Shepherds said the Basajaun would whistle from the trees to warn them when storms or wolves threatened their flocks.
humanoids.story3: One tale claims humans secretly watched the Basajaun to steal the secrets of the saw, the axle, and welding iron.

quiz.question: What is the Basajaun often called in Basque folklore?

humanoids.name: Abarimon
humanoids.physical: A savage race of humans whose feet are turned backward, yet who run with astonishing speed.
humanoids.stories: Ancient writers placed the Abarimon in a remote Himalayan valley, where they lived among wild beasts and could not survive the air of any other land.
humanoids.symbolism: The Abarimon embody the ancient fascination with monstrous peoples said to dwell at the edges of the known world.
humanoids.powers: Great swiftness despite reversed feet, and the ability to live wild alongside dangerous animals.
humanoids.modernRefs: Medieval bestiaries, fantasy worldbuilding, mythical race compendiums.
humanoids.story2: Pliny the Elder wrote that the Abarimon could never be captured alive, for they died if taken from their native air.
humanoids.story3: Their backward feet, folklore claims, made their trails impossible to follow, confusing any hunter who tracked them.

quiz.question: What unusual feature defines the Abarimon?

humanoids.name: Water Babies
humanoids.physical: Spirits dwelling near springs and ponds that appear as wailing infants to lure the unwary.
humanoids.stories: A water baby cries near a pool, and when a kind person lifts the seeming infant it grows impossibly heavy, dragging or crushing the victim into the water.
humanoids.symbolism: Water Babies warn of the hidden dangers of deep water and the peril of acting on misplaced pity.
humanoids.powers: Deceptive infant cries, sudden crushing weight, and command over their watery homes.
humanoids.modernRefs: Regional ghost lore, cryptid podcasts, Indigenous storytelling.
humanoids.story2: Elders cautioned children never to approach crying sounds near isolated springs, lest a water baby be waiting.
humanoids.story3: Some traditions hold that the cry of a water baby foretells a death or misfortune to come.

quiz.question: How does a Water Baby lure its victims?

humanoids.name: Werecoyote
humanoids.physical: A canine therianthrope able to shift between human form and that of a coyote.
humanoids.stories: Tied to the trickster spirit of the coyote, the werecoyote slips between human and beast, weaving mischief, cunning, and the occasional dark sorcery.
humanoids.symbolism: The werecoyote captures the coyote's role as a clever, unpredictable trickster who crosses every boundary.
humanoids.powers: Shapeshifting between human and coyote, cunning, stealth, and trickster magic.
humanoids.modernRefs: Urban fantasy series, werewolf media, Indigenous folklore studies.
humanoids.story2: Stories say a werecoyote laughs as it tricks travelers, leading them in circles through the desert night.
humanoids.story3: Some tales blur the werecoyote with the skin-walker, a witch who dons a coyote's hide to do harm.

quiz.question: Which animal is the werecoyote linked to as a trickster?

humanoids.name: Tonttu
humanoids.physical: A small, bearded dwarf-like household spirit, often clad in grey with a pointed red cap.
humanoids.stories: A tonttu guards a farm and home, doing quiet chores by night, but it grows wrathful if the household is lazy, unclean, or disrespectful.
humanoids.symbolism: The tonttu embodies the rewards of diligence and the old bond between a family and the spirit of its home.
humanoids.powers: Household protection, good fortune for the farm, and stern punishment for the careless.
humanoids.modernRefs: Nordic Christmas traditions, the Joulutonttu (Christmas elf), folklore festivals.
humanoids.story2: Farmers left a bowl of porridge with butter for the tonttu at Christmas to keep its favor through the year.
humanoids.story3: Tales warn that a slighted tonttu may tangle the livestock, sour the milk, or abandon the farm to ruin.

quiz.question: What does a tonttu expect from the household it guards?

sections.title: Birth of Mythology
sections.subtitle: How and why myths were created

topics.name: Origins of Myths
topics.description: Myths arose from humanity's earliest attempts to explain nature, danger, death, weather, and social order.
topics.examples: Creation myths, flood narratives, sun and moon personifications.

topics.name: Oral Traditions
topics.description: Before writing, stories preserved genealogy, law, ethics, memory, and ecological knowledge.
topics.examples: Epics, Dreamtime stories, Vedic oral hymns.

topics.name: Religion & Mythology
topics.description: In ancient cultures, myth and religion were often inseparable frameworks for understanding the world.
topics.examples: Greek pantheon, Shinto kami, Djinn in Islamic theology.

topics.name: Folklore
topics.description: Community stories explained local landscapes, household customs, seasonal danger, and moral behavior.
topics.examples: European fairy lore, Japanese yokai, Native American spirit stories.

topics.name: Forest Myths
topics.description: Forest beings personified mystery, danger, medicine, and hidden knowledge.

topics.name: Mountain Spirits
topics.description: Mountains were treated as thresholds between human life and divine or dangerous powers.
topics.examples: Tengu, Olympian gods, Tibetan mountain deities.

traits.name: Fluid Identity
traits.description: The ability to change form reflects unstable boundaries between human, animal, and spirit.
traits.examples: Fox brides, hidden spirits, animal-human transformations.

traits.name: Forest Wisdom
traits.description: Nature spirits preserve herbal, ecological, and seasonal knowledge.
traits.examples: Healing herbs, sacred groves, animal guides.

traits.name: Hidden Builders
traits.description: Mythic builders create impossible objects and structures outside ordinary time.

collections.title: Shape-Shifting Across Cultures
collections.description: From Kitsune to Djinn, transformation myths reveal how cultures think about identity, nature, and spirit boundaries.

collections.title: Sacred Forest Spirits
collections.description: Forest beings encode ecological knowledge, taboo, healing, and the sacred force of wilderness.

collections.title: Ancient Builders
collections.description: Builder beings preserve memories of craft, stonework, older peoples, and supernatural labor.

collections.title: Giants & Hybrid Beings
collections.description: Part-human, part-animal giants from Greek myth embody the tension between civilization, strength, and wild instinct.

collections.title: Omens & Death Spirits
collections.description: Beings who herald or govern death, from wailing messengers to those who choose the fate of warriors.

collections.title: Water & Serpent Spirits
collections.description: Water-dwelling humanoids across Asia and Africa who govern fertility, wealth, and the dangers of the deep.

glossary.definition: The belief that animals, plants, rivers, mountains, and objects possess spiritual essence.
glossary.example: Forest spirits and sacred groves appear in many animistic traditions.

glossary.definition: A recurring character type or symbolic pattern found across cultures.
glossary.example: The Trickster appears as Kitsune, Loki, Anansi, and Coyote.

glossary.definition: A threshold state between worlds, identities, or phases of life.
glossary.example: Kitsune and Djinn often cross the boundary between human and spirit worlds.
